questions.questionText: Febrile seizures in children typically occur between:
questions.optionB: 6 months to 5 years (triggered by fever ≥38°C, most common seizure disorder in children, simple type: <15 min, generalized, non-recurrent in 24h)
questions.explanation: Febrile seizures: most common seizure disorder in children (2-5% of children). Age: 6 months - 5 years (peak 12-18 months). Simple febrile seizure (75%): generalized tonic-clonic, <15 minutes, single in 24 hours, no focal features, no post-ictal deficit. Complex: focal onset, >15 min, recurrent in 24h, post-ictal Todd's paralysis. Recurrence risk: 30% (higher if: <18 months, family history, low-grade fever at seizure onset). Risk of epilepsy: simple (1-2% — same as general population), complex (4-6%). Treatment: acute → rectal diazepam/buccal midazolam. Prophylaxis usually NOT indicated for simple FS. Reference: OP Ghai — Febrile Seizures.

questions.questionText: West syndrome (infantile spasms) is characterized by the triad of:
questions.optionB: Infantile spasms (flexor/extensor), hypsarrhythmia on EEG, and developmental regression
questions.explanation: West syndrome: severe epileptic encephalopathy of infancy (peak 4-7 months). Triad: (1) Infantile spasms — clusters of flexor (salaam attacks/jackknife), extensor, or mixed spasms, often on awakening, (2) Hypsarrhythmia — chaotic, high-amplitude, disorganized EEG pattern (pathognomonic), (3) Developmental regression/arrest. Causes: prenatal (tuberous sclerosis — most important association, cortical malformations), perinatal (HIE), postnatal (infections). Treatment: ACTH/prednisolone (first-line), vigabatrin (especially for tuberous sclerosis). Poor prognosis: may evolve to Lennox-Gastaut syndrome. Reference: OP Ghai — West Syndrome.
